verbatim_text: And then we find in all cases, self-explaner beats another model as explainer. So yes, privileged access also holds. OK, so together these two results mean that we found empirical evidence that language models trained to explain themselves learn to use self-models across all three domains. And finally, what are the practical benefits of this? So we hypothesized that there might be some data efficiency benefits for using self-models. And to answer this, we're going to train various external interpretability techniques, which are going to be the orange, green, and red lines and the self-models technique. And we're going to look at what happens when we expose them to varying amounts of training data. So this nearest neighbor approach at a high level, it retrieves the closest feature in a feature dictionary. This is kind of a rough proxy for other common interpret techniques. So our results are here, and you can see that a model trained to explain itself performs about equivalently to another model trained on 10 times more features. And it performs about equivalently to a retrieval-based method that uses 100 times more data. OK, so basically what this plot is intended to show is that existing interpretability techniques common have a trade-off between scalability and faithfulness. So remember, we had the approach of directly asking the model, which was highly scalable but unfaithful. Then we had all these really fine-grained interpretability tools, which were highly faithful but not scalable.
